1.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 1
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God commands Hosea to marry an unfaithful wife as a living parable of Israel's spiritual adultery, and their three children receive prophetic names declaring judgment — yet the chapter closes with a stunning promise of future restoration.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Narrative — God's Marriage Command
Key Themes: Spiritual adultery of Israel, God's judgment through
symbolic names, the coming restoration despite unfaithfulness,
covenant love that persists through betrayal

Historical Context: Hosea's ministry spans the reigns of four
Judean kings (Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah) and Jeroboam II
of Israel — a period of material prosperity but spiritual decay.
God commands Hosea to marry Gomer, a woman of "whoredoms," as a
living parable of God's relationship with Israel. The nation has
"departed from the LORD" (v.2) — chasing Baal worship and pagan
alliances while abandoning covenant faithfulness. The three
children's names are prophetic pronouncements of judgment: Jezreel
(God scatters), Lo-ruhamah (No mercy), and Lo-ammi (Not my
people). Yet even here, the chapter ends with a stunning reversal
— future restoration and reunion under "one head."

Structure:
- The Prophetic Commission: Hosea's Marriage (vv.1-3)
- First Child: Jezreel — Judgment on Jehu's House (vv.4-5)
- Second Child: Lo-ruhamah — No Mercy (vv.6-7)
- Third Child: Lo-ammi — Not My People (vv.8-9)
- The Promise of Future Restoration (vv.10-11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Jeremiah    | 3       | 6-14               | God as the betrayed husband; Israel as the faithless wife who commits adultery with idols
v.2              | Ezekiel     | 16      | 15-34              | Extended metaphor of Israel as an unfaithful wife lavishing God's gifts on other lovers
v.4              | 2 Kings     | 9       | 14                 | 10:11 — The bloodshed of Jezreel by Jehu
v.6              | 1 Peter     | 2       | 10                 | "Once you were not a people, but now you are God's people; once you had not received mercy, but now you have received mercy." (ESV)
v.9              | Exodus      | 6       | 7                  | "I will take you to be my people, and I will be your God" (ESV) — the original covenant formula now reversed
v.10             | Romans      | 9       | 25-26              | Paul applies this passage to Gentile believers: "Those who were not my people I will call 'my people.'" (ESV)
v.10             | Genesis     | 22      | 17                 | "As the sand that is on the seashore" (ESV) — the Abrahamic promise still stands
v.11             | Ezekiel     | 37      | 22                 | "I will make them one nation... and one king shall be king over them all." (ESV)
v.11             | Ephesians   | 2       | 14                 | Christ "who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "wife of whoredoms" — Hebrew "eshet zenunim." Debated: was Gomer already a prostitute or did she become one? The plural "whoredoms" suggests a pattern/disposition. Either way, the prophetic point remains: God knowingly married Himself to a people He knew would be unfaithful.
v.4              | KJV         | "Jezreel" = "God sows" or "God scatters." Double meaning: judgment (scattering in exile) and future hope (sowing for a harvest). The Valley of Jezreel was a famous battlefield.
v.6              | KJV         | "Lo-ruhamah" = "Not pitied" or "No mercy." Hebrew root "racham" = womb-love, deep compassion. The negation of this tender word is devastating.
v.9              | KJV         | "Lo-ammi" = "Not my people." The reversal of covenant identity. In Hebrew: "lo ammi attem, v'anochi lo ehyeh lachem" — literally "not-my-people you, and I not-I-AM to you." The divine name "I AM" (Exodus 3:14) is being withdrawn from the relationship.
v.10             | KJV         | "sons of the living God" — Hebrew "bnei El-chai." The contrast is powerful: the "dead" idols they pursued vs. the "living God" who will reclaim them.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God uses painful experiences as prophetic pictures (v.2). Hosea didn't
   just preach about God's heartbreak — he LIVED it. Your own painful
   experiences may be the very thing God uses to give you authority and
   compassion in ministry. Pain is not wasted.

2. Names matter — they declare identity and destiny (vv.4-9). God named each
   child as a prophetic word to the nation. What "names" has the world
   spoken over you? What names has GOD spoken? The chapter ends with God
   RENAMING: "Not my people" becomes "sons of the living God." God is the
   ultimate name-giver.

3. Sin has consequences even when forgiveness is available (vv.4-9).
   Judgment came. The names were real. Israel DID fall. God's patience is
   not permission; His mercy is not indifference. There is a point where
   consequences must come — not from anger, but from love that refuses to
   enable destruction.

4. Grace always gets the last word (vv.10-11). The chapter begins with
   judgment names and ends with restoration promises. This is the pattern of
   Scripture: judgment is NEVER God's final word for His people. Even in the
   darkest pronouncement, hope breaks through. If God can restore "Not my
   people" to "sons of the living God," there is no one beyond His reach.

5. "One head" (v.11) — Reunion under Christ. Divided Israel, divided
   humanity, Jew and Gentile — all gathered under one King. Whatever
   divisions exist now are temporary. God's plan is UNITY in Christ.

------------------------------------

David Guzik — Hosea 1 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/hosea-1/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

2.ESV:
1. Say to your brothers, “You are my people,” and to your sisters, “You have received mercy.”
2. “Plead with your mother, plead— for she is not my wife, and I am not her husband— that she put away her whoring from her face, and her adultery from between her breasts;
3. lest I strip her naked and make her as in the day she was born, and make her like a wilderness, and make her like a parched land, and kill her with thirst.
4. Upon her children also I will have no mercy, because they are children of whoredom.
5. For their mother has played the whore; she who conceived them has acted shamefully. For she said, ‘I will go after my lovers, who give me my bread and my water, my wool and my flax, my oil and my drink.’
6. Therefore I will hedge up her way with thorns, and I will build a wall against her, so that she cannot find her paths.
7. She shall pursue her lovers but not overtake them, and she shall seek them but shall not find them. Then she shall say, ‘I will go and return to my first husband, for it was better for me then than now.’
8. And she did not know that it was I who gave her the grain, the wine, and the oil, and who lavished on her silver and gold, which they used for Baal.
9. Therefore I will take back my grain in its time, and my wine in its season, and I will take away my wool and my flax, which were to cover her nakedness.
10. Now I will uncover her lewdness in the sight of her lovers, and no one shall rescue her out of my hand.
11. And I will put an end to all her mirth, her feasts, her new moons, her Sabbaths, and all her appointed feasts.
12. And I will lay waste her vines and her fig trees, of which she said, ‘These are my wages, which my lovers have given me.’ I will make them a forest, and the beasts of the field shall devour them.
13. And I will punish her for the feast days of the Baals when she burned offerings to them and adorned herself with her ring and jewelry, and went after her lovers and forgot me, declares the LORD.
14. “Therefore, behold, I will allure her, and bring her into the wilderness, and speak tenderly to her.
15. And there I will give her her vineyards and make the Valley of Achor a door of hope. And there she shall answer as in the days of her youth, as at the time when she came out of the land of Egypt.
16. “And in that day, declares the LORD, you will call me ‘My Husband,’ and no longer will you call me ‘My Baal.’
17. For I will remove the names of the Baals from her mouth, and they shall be remembered by name no more.
18. And I will make for them a covenant on that day with the beasts of the field, the birds of the heavens, and the creeping things of the ground. And I will abolish the bow, the sword, and war from the land, and I will make you lie down in safety.
19. And I will betroth you to me forever. I will betroth you to me in righteousness and in justice, in steadfast love and in mercy.
20. I will betroth you to me in faithfulness. And you shall know the LORD.
21. “And in that day I will answer, declares the LORD, I will answer the heavens, and they shall answer the earth,
22. and the earth shall answer the grain, the wine, and the oil, and they shall answer Jezreel,
23. and I will sow her for myself in the land. And I will have mercy on No Mercy, and I will say to Not My People, ‘You are my people’; and he shall say, ‘You are my God.’”

9. Therefore, I will take back my grain during the harvest time and my new wine when it ripens; I will take away my wool and my flax that I had provided in order to clothe her.
10. Soon I will expose her lewd nakedness in front of her lovers, and no one will be able to rescue her from me!
11. I will put an end to all her celebrations: her annual religious festivals, monthly new moon celebrations, and weekly Sabbath festivities— all her appointed festivals.
12. I will destroy her vines and fig trees, about which she said, ‘These are my wages for prostitution that my lovers gave to me!’ I will turn her cultivated vines and fig trees into an uncultivated thicket, so that wild animals will devour them.
13. I will punish her for the festival days when she burned incense to the Baal idols; she adorned herself with earrings and jewelry, and went after her lovers, but she forgot me!” says the Lord.
14. “However, in the future I will allure her; I will lead her back into the wilderness and speak tenderly to her.
15. From there I will give back her vineyards to her and turn the ‘Valley of Trouble’ into an ‘Opportunity for Hope.’ There she will sing as she did when she was young, when she came up from the land of Egypt.

2.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 2
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God declares judgment on unfaithful Israel (His adulterous wife) by stripping away her blessings, yet promises to allure her back to Himself and restore the covenant relationship with tenderness and everlasting love.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Unfaithful Wife and the
Pursuing Husband
Key Themes: Spiritual adultery exposed, the discipline of love,
God's relentless pursuit, covenant renewal and betrothal, the
reversal of judgment names

Historical Context: Chapter 2 expands the marriage metaphor of
chapter 1 into a full prophetic oracle. Israel is the unfaithful
wife who credits her "lovers" (Baals/pagan gods) for the blessings
that actually came from God (v.8). She pursues other gods for
bread, water, wool, flax, oil, and wine — all things GOD provided.
The chapter moves in three movements: accusation (vv.1-5),
discipline (vv.6-13), and restoration (vv.14-23). The turning
point is the extraordinary "Therefore" of v.14 — where we expect
final judgment, God instead speaks of ALLURING her back. This is
the heart of Hosea: God's love that refuses to let go.

Structure:
- The Accusation Against the Unfaithful Wife (vv.1-5)
- God's Discipline: Hedging Her Way with Thorns (vv.6-13)
- The Stunning Reversal: Alluring Love (vv.14-15)
- The New Covenant: Betrothal Forever (vv.16-20)
- The Great Restoration: Reversal of Judgment Names (vv.21-23)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.6              | Luke        | 15      | 14-17              | The Prodigal Son brought to the end of himself before returning to the father
v.7              | Jeremiah    | 2       | 13                 | "My people have committed two evils: they have forsaken me, the fountain of living waters, and hewed out cisterns for themselves, broken cisterns that can hold no water." (ESV)
v.8              | James       | 1       | 17                 | "Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above." (ESV) All blessings originate with God alone
v.14             | Song of Solomon | 2       | 14                 | God as the pursuing lover who draws His beloved into intimacy
v.15             | Joshua      | 7       | 24-26              | The Valley of Achor: place of Achan's judgment. Now transformed into hope
v.16             | Galatians   | 4       | 6-7                | No longer slaves but sons; not "master" language but "Abba, Father."
v.19             | Isaiah      | 54      | 5                  | "For your Maker is your husband, the LORD of hosts is his name." (ESV)
v.19             | Ephesians   | 5       | 25-27              | Christ as the husband who cleanses and presents His bride holy
v.23             | Romans      | 9       | 25-26              | Paul applies this directly to Gentile inclusion in the people of God
v.23             | 1 Peter     | 2       | 10                 | "Once you were not a people, but now you are God's people; once you had not received mercy, but now you have received mercy." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | has "Ammi" and "Ru-hamah" (reversing the "Lo-" prefix), while ESV reads "You are my people" and "You have received mercy." Same meaning — the judgment names of ch.1 are undone.
v.14             | KJV         | "allure" — Hebrew "patah" = to persuade, entice, seduce (in a positive sense here). God uses the language of romantic pursuit. He will WIN her back through tenderness, not force.
v.14             | KJV         | "speak comfortably" (KJV) / "speak tenderly" (ESV) — Hebrew "dibber al-leb" = literally "speak upon her heart." Heart-language, not head-language. Intimacy, not lecture.
v.15             | KJV         | "Achor" = "trouble" (from "achar," to trouble). The valley where Achan was judged (Joshua 7). God takes the site of judgment and makes it a threshold of restoration.
v.16             | KJV         | "Ishi" = "my man/husband" (personal, intimate). "Baali" = "my owner/master" (also sounds like "my Baal"). God wants to be loved, not just obeyed out of servile fear.
v.19             | KJV         | "betroth" — Hebrew "aras" = to betroth with a bride-price. God PAYS to get His wife back. This anticipates the cross: redemption costs God everything.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. We often credit the wrong source for our blessings (v.8). Like Israel
   giving Baal credit for God's grain, wine, and oil — do you attribute your
   success to your own effort, luck, or systems while forgetting that EVERY
   good gift comes from God? Check where your gratitude actually flows.

2. God's "thorns" are acts of love (v.6). When God blocks your path to sin —
   making it harder, more painful, less satisfying — that is mercy. The
   frustration you feel when sin stops "working" is God hedging you in with
   thorns to protect you from yourself.

3. The wilderness is where God speaks tenderly (v.14). God doesn't allure
   Israel in the palace but in the WILDERNESS — the place of stripping,
   quietness, and dependence. Your wilderness season may be the very place
   God intends to speak most intimately to you. Don't despise the desert.

4. "A door of hope" in the valley of trouble (v.15). Your worst failure,
   your deepest shame — God can transform it into the gateway of your
   greatest breakthrough. Achor became a door. Your "Achor" can too.

5. God wants intimacy, not servility (v.16). He doesn't want you to call Him
   "Master" out of duty — He wants you to call Him "Husband" out of love.
   Christianity is not religious obligation; it is relational intimacy with
   the living God.

6. "I will betroth thee unto me FOREVER" (v.19). God doesn't just forgive —
   He REMARRIES. He doesn't just tolerate — He BETROTHS. The unfaithful wife
   doesn't get probation; she gets a wedding. This is the scandal of grace.

------------------------------------

3.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 3
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God commands Hosea to buy back and love his adulterous wife again, symbolizing God's redemptive love for Israel — though they must endure a period of discipline before full restoration.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Narrative — Redemption of the Adulterous
Wife
Key Themes: Redeeming love, the cost of restoration, the long
waiting period, future return to the LORD and David's King

Historical Context: Chapter 3 is the most intensely personal
chapter in Hosea — only 5 verses, yet containing one of the most
powerful pictures of redemption in the Old Testament. God commands
Hosea to go AGAIN and love his wife who has left him for another
man. She has apparently sunk so low that she must be PURCHASED
back — bought at the slave market for the price of a common slave
(30 shekels total value: 15 silver + barley equivalent). This is
Hosea's calvary: buying back at great cost what was rightfully his.
The chapter then telescopes into prophecy: Israel will endure a
LONG period without king, sacrifice, or religious identity (the
exile and diaspora), but "afterward" they will return and seek
the LORD and "David their king" — the Messiah.

Structure:
- God's Command: Love Her Again (v.1)
- The Purchase: Buying Back the Adulterous Wife (v.2)
- The Condition: Faithful Waiting (v.3)
- The Prophetic Parallel: Israel's Long Exile (v.4)
- The Promise: Return in the Latter Days (v.5)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Romans      | 5       | 8                  | "While we were still sinners, Christ died for us." (ESV) God loves BEFORE repentance
v.1              | Ephesians   | 5       | 25                 | "Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her." (ESV)
v.2              | Exodus      | 21      | 32                 | Thirty shekels: the price of a slave. The redemption price parallels Christ's betrayal price
v.2              | Matthew     | 26      | 15                 | Judas receives 30 pieces of silver for betraying Jesus. Same slave-price
v.2              | 1 Peter     | 1       | 18-19              | "You were ransomed...not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ." (ESV)
v.4              | Luke        | 21      | 24                 | "Jerusalem will be trampled underfoot by the Gentiles, until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled." (ESV)
v.5              | Ezekiel     | 34      | 23-24              | "I will set up over them one shepherd, my servant David, and he shall feed them." (ESV)
v.5              | Jeremiah    | 30      | 9                  | "They shall serve the LORD their God and David their king, whom I will raise up for them." (ESV)
v.5              | Romans      | 11      | 25-26              | "A partial hardening has come upon Israel, until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in. And in this way all Israel will be saved." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "beloved of her friend" (KJV) / "loved by another man" (ESV) — The Hebrew "re'a" can mean friend, companion, or lover. ESV clarifies: she is with another man. Hosea must love her WHILE she belongs to someone else.
v.1              | KJV         | "flagons of wine" (KJV) / "cakes of raisins" (ESV) — Hebrew "ashishah" = raisin cakes, used in pagan fertility worship (cf. Song 2:5, 2 Samuel 6:19). The KJV's "flagons of wine" is less precise; ESV correctly identifies the pagan ritual food offerings.
v.2              | KJV         | "fifteen pieces of silver, and for an homer of barley, and an half homer of barley" — The total equals approximately 30 shekels (the barley being worth about 15 shekels). This was the price of a slave (Exodus 21:32). Hosea doesn't even have enough silver — he must supplement with grain. He gives everything he has.
v.4              | KJV         | "without an image" (KJV) / "without pillar" (ESV) — Hebrew "matstsebah" = sacred pillar/standing stone. Listed alongside legitimate items (king, sacrifice, ephod), suggesting Israel loses BOTH true worship and false worship — left in a void.
v.5              | KJV         | "David their king" — Not literal David (long dead) but the Messianic King from David's line. This is standard prophetic shorthand for the Messiah (cf. Jeremiah 30:9, Ezekiel 37:24).

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Go AGAIN, love" (v.1). The hardest command in Scripture is not "love
   your enemies" in the abstract — it is "love THAT person again." The one
   who betrayed you. The one who left. God calls Hosea to love again — not
   because Gomer deserves it, but "according to the love of the LORD." Our
   model for love is not human capacity — it is divine character.

2. Redemption has a price (v.2). Hosea paid silver and barley. Christ paid
   blood. You were bought at a price (1 Corinthians 6:20). Salvation is free
   to you but it cost God EVERYTHING. Never treat lightly what cost so much.

3. After redemption comes discipline (v.3). Being bought back doesn't mean
   instant freedom to do whatever you want. There is a season of learning
   faithfulness, of waiting, of being still. Sanctification follows
   justification. Let God's restoration process unfold at HIS pace.

4. The long silence has a purpose (v.4). Israel's "many days" without king
   or sacrifice is not God's abandonment — it is the waiting period before
   "afterward." If you are in a season where God seems absent, where the
   structures you relied on are gone — take heart. "Afterward" is coming.

5. "In the latter days" (v.5). God's plan is not finished. The full story
   has not been told. Israel's restoration, the Messiah's reign, the fear of
   the LORD covering the earth — these are coming. Live with "latter days"
   vision. The present is not the conclusion.

------------------------------------

David Guzik — Hosea 3 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/hosea-3/

BibleProject Podcast:

4.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God brings a legal charge against Israel for their lack of faithfulness, knowledge, and rampant idolatry — the priests have failed to teach, and the people are destroyed for lack of knowledge.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — God's Case Against Israel
Key Themes: God's lawsuit against His people, failed priesthood,
destruction through ignorance, the spirit of whoredom, like
people like priest, creation groaning under human sin

Historical Context: Chapter 4 begins the second major section of
Hosea (chs.4-14), moving from personal narrative (Hosea's marriage)
to national indictment. God brings a formal "controversy" (Hebrew
"rib" = legal case/lawsuit) against Israel. The charges read like
a reversal of the Ten Commandments: no truth, no mercy, no
knowledge of God — instead, swearing, lying, killing, stealing,
adultery. The priests — who should have taught God's law — have
themselves rejected knowledge. The result: the entire nation
perishes in ignorance, and even creation suffers.

Structure:
- God's Lawsuit: The Charges (vv.1-3)
- Indictment of the Priesthood (vv.4-9)
- The Futility of Idolatry (vv.10-14)
- Warning to Judah: Don't Follow Israel (vv.15-19)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Micah       | 6       | 2                  | "The LORD has a controversy with his people." (ESV) Same legal-case language
v.2              | Exodus      | 20      | 1-17               | The Ten Commandments systematically violated
v.3              | Romans      | 8       | 20-22              | Creation groaning under the curse of human sin
v.6              | 2 Chronicles | 15      | 3                  | "For a long time Israel was without the true God, and without a teaching priest." (ESV)
v.6              | Malachi     | 2       | 7                  | "For the lips of a priest should guard knowledge, and people should seek instruction from his mouth." (ESV)
v.9              | Isaiah      | 24      | 2                  | "And it shall be, as with the people, so with the priest." (ESV)
v.12             | Isaiah      | 44      | 19-20              | The absurdity of worshipping wood
v.14             | Numbers     | 25      | 1-3                | Israel yoked to Baal of Peor through sexual immorality and idolatry
v.17             | Romans      | 1       | 24,                | 26, 28 — "God gave them up" (ESV) — the NT equivalent of "leave him alone.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "controversy" — Hebrew "rib" = a legal dispute, lawsuit. God is not merely angry; He is formally prosecuting Israel as a covenant violator.
v.6              | KJV         | "destroyed" — Hebrew "damah" = cut off, silenced, destroyed. "Knowledge" — Hebrew "da'at" = intimate knowing (same word as "Adam knew Eve," Genesis 4:1). Not just intellectual information but relational intimacy with God.
v.12             | KJV         | "stocks" (KJV) / "piece of wood" (ESV) — wooden idols. "Staff declareth" = using wooden rods for divination (a form of the occult), replacing prayer with superstition.
v.15             | KJV         | "Gilgal" and "Beth-aven" — Gilgal: formerly sacred (Joshua's circumcision/memorial), now corrupted into a pagan worship site. "Beth-aven" = "house of wickedness" — Hosea's derogatory name for Bethel ("house of God"), which had become a center of calf worship.
v.16             | KJV         | "backsliding heifer" (KJV) / "stubborn heifer" (ESV) — Hebrew "sorerah" = stubborn, rebellious. Israel is like a cow that refuses the yoke. God cannot lead the unwilling.
v.17             | KJV         | "joined" — Hebrew "chabur" = bound, allied. Ephraim is FUSED to idols — inseparable. The attachment is so deep that only God's releasing them to consequences will break it.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Destroyed for lack of knowledge" (v.6). This is not ignorance from lack
   of opportunity — it is REJECTED knowledge. The information was available;
   they chose not to receive it. Are you actively pursuing knowledge of God,
   or are Bibles sitting unread, sermons unheared, and truth unconsidered?
   Destruction comes not from unavailability but from refusal.

2. Failed leaders produce failed people (v.9). "Like people, like priest."
   If you are in any position of spiritual influence — parent, teacher,
   pastor, friend — your condition shapes others. The priests rejected
   knowledge, and the NATION was destroyed. Leadership is not optional
   responsibility.

3. The "spirit of whoredom" is real (v.12). Spiritual unfaithfulness is not
   just occasional failure — it can become a controlling disposition. When
   you consistently choose substitutes for God, a SPIRIT of idolatry takes
   root. The solution is not behavior modification but spiritual
   deliverance.

4. "Let him alone" — the terror of being released to your own choices
   (v.17). There is something worse than God's discipline: God's silence.
   When God stops pursuing, stops blocking, stops chastening — THAT is the
   most dangerous place. If you still feel conviction, be grateful. It means
   God has NOT let you alone.

5. Creation suffers when people sin (v.3). Human rebellion doesn't just
   destroy the sinner — it corrupts the land, the animals, the ecology. Sin
   is never private. Your choices affect more than you know.

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/hosea-4/

5.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God pronounces judgment on the priests, people, and royal house of Israel for their pride and spiritual prostitution, declaring He will withdraw until they acknowledge their guilt and seek His face.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — Judgment on Both Israel and Judah
Key Themes: God as a lion and a moth, failed alliances with Assyria,
pride as the root of destruction, God withdrawing until repentance,
seeking God in distress

Historical Context: Chapter 5 expands the indictment to include
both Israel (Ephraim) and Judah. The priests, the royal house,
and the people are all guilty. God has become like a "moth" to
Ephraim (slow, invisible decay) and will become like a "lion"
(sudden, devastating judgment). The chapter references the
Syro-Ephraimite crisis (approximately 735 BC), when Ephraim sought
help from Assyria rather than God (v.13) — a political alliance
that would ultimately destroy them. The chapter ends with God
"returning to His place" — withdrawing divine presence until
Israel genuinely repents.

Structure:
- Triple Indictment: Priests, Israel, Royal House (vv.1-7)
- The Alarm: War is Coming (vv.8-9)
- Judah Also Condemned (v.10)
- God as Moth and Lion (vv.11-14)
- God Withdraws Until Repentance (v.15)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | Jeremiah    | 13      | 23                 | "Can the Ethiopian change his skin or the leopard his spots? Then also you can do good who are accustomed to do evil." (ESV)
v.6              | Proverbs    | 1       | 28                 | "Then they will call upon me, but I will not answer; they will seek me diligently but will not find me." (ESV)
v.6              | Isaiah      | 1       | 11-15              | God rejecting Israel's sacrifices while their hearts remain far from Him
v.12             | Job         | 13      | 28                 | "Man wastes away like a rotten thing, like a garment that is moth-eaten." (ESV)
v.13             | 2 Kings     | 15      | 19-20              | Menahem paying tribute to Assyria
v.13             | Isaiah      | 30      | 1-3                | "Woe to the rebellious children...who set out to go down to Egypt." (ESV)
v.14             | Lamentations | 3       | 10                 | "He is a bear lying in wait for me, a lion in hiding." (ESV)
v.15             | 2 Chronicles | 15      | 4                  | "But when in their distress they turned to the LORD...and sought him, he was found by them." (ESV)
v.15             | Jeremiah    | 29      | 13                 | "You will seek me and find me, when you seek me with all your heart." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Mizpah... Tabor" — Mizpah (east of Jordan) and Mount Tabor (in Jezreel Valley) were sites where leaders set traps for the people — likely pagan worship centers or places of corruption. The leaders ensnared the people at sacred sites.
v.8              | KJV         | "Gibeah... Ramah... Beth-aven" — Alarm cities along the border between Ephraim and Benjamin. The warning sounds north to south — invasion is coming.
v.10             | KJV         | "remove the bound" (KJV) / "move the landmark" (ESV) — Hebrew "masig gebul." Moving boundary stones was a grave sin (Deuteronomy 19:14). Judah took advantage of Israel's weakness to seize territory.
v.12             | KJV         | "moth" — Hebrew "ash" = clothes moth, which destroys slowly from within, unnoticed until the garment falls apart. "Rottenness" (KJV) / "dry rot" (ESV) — Hebrew "raqab" = decay in wood/bone. Silent, internal destruction.
v.13             | KJV         | "king Jareb" (KJV) / "the great king" (ESV) — "Jareb" likely means "the warrior/contender king." Probably a title for the Assyrian king rather than a proper name.
v.15             | KJV         | "acknowledge their offence" — Hebrew "asham" = guilt/ trespass. God doesn't merely want sorrow — He wants ACKNOWLEDGMENT of guilt. Confession precedes restoration.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Pride "testifies to his face" (v.5). Pride is always visible to everyone
   except the one who has it. If pride is testifying against you, ask God
   and trusted friends: where am I blind to my own arrogance? Humility is
   the gateway to repentance.

2. Religious activity without genuine heart finds God absent (v.6). You can
   bring your "flocks and herds" — attend church, give money, serve — and
   still find God has withdrawn. He is not impressed by ritual. He responds
   to authenticity. Check whether your spiritual activity is reaching God or
   just performing for people.

3. God sometimes works like a moth before He works like a lion (vv.12, 14).
   Slow decay precedes sudden collapse. If you sense quiet deterioration in
   your spiritual life — passion fading, conviction weakening, prayer
   growing cold — address it NOW. The moth stage is your warning. Don't wait
   for the lion.

4. Human solutions cannot fix God-problems (v.13). Ephraim's wound was FROM
   God (spiritual sickness), but they sought healing FROM Assyria (political
   alliance). When your problem is with God, no therapist, no friend, no
   career change can heal it. Only returning to God addresses the actual
   issue.

5. "In their affliction they will seek me early" (v.15). God allows
   affliction because He knows it produces genuine seeking. If you are in
   affliction now — SEEK. This may be exactly why God permitted it. Don't
   waste your pain.

------------------------------------

David Guzik — Hosea 5 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/hosea-5/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

6.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 6
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Israel offers superficial repentance — their love is like morning dew that quickly vanishes — and God declares He desires steadfast love and knowledge of Himself rather than mere sacrifices.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — Superficial Repentance and
God's Desire for Steadfast Love
Key Themes: False repentance that evaporates, the third-day
resurrection, God's desire for mercy over sacrifice, the knowledge
of God, covenant breaking like Adam

Historical Context: Chapter 6 opens with what appears to be a
beautiful call to repentance (vv.1-3), but God's response (v.4)
reveals it as shallow. Their "goodness" (hesed/love) evaporates
like morning dew. This is the tragedy of Israel: not the absence
of religious feeling, but its SHALLOWNESS. They come to God with
emotion but without substance. God responds with one of the most
quoted verses in the prophets: "I desired mercy, and not sacrifice"
(v.6) — quoted by Jesus twice (Matthew 9:13, 12:7). The chapter
reveals that God's primary desire is not ritual but RELATIONSHIP.

Structure:
- Israel's Call to Repentance (vv.1-3)
- God's Lament: Your Love is Like Morning Dew (v.4)
- God's True Desire: Mercy, Not Sacrifice (vv.5-6)
- The Catalogue of Crimes (vv.7-11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | 1 Corinthians | 15      | 4                  | "He was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures." (ESV)
v.2              | Luke        | 24      | 46                 | "Thus it is written, that the Christ should suffer and on the third day rise from the dead." (ESV)
v.4              | Matthew     | 13      | 20-21              | The seed on rocky ground: "he hears the word and immediately receives it with joy, yet he has no root...but endures for a while." (ESV)
v.6              | Matthew     | 9       | 13                 | Jesus says, "Go and learn what this means: 'I desire mercy, and not sacrifice.'" (ESV)
v.6              | Matthew     | 12      | 7                  | Jesus again: "If you had known what this means, 'I desire mercy, and not sacrifice,' you would not have condemned the guiltless." (ESV)
v.6              | 1 Samuel    | 15      | 22                 | "Has the LORD as great delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices, as in obeying the voice of the LORD? Behold, to obey is better than sacrifice." (ESV)
v.6              | Micah       | 6       | 6-8                | "What does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness?" (ESV)
v.7              | Genesis     | 3       | 6-11               | Adam's original covenant-breaking
v.7              | Romans      | 5       | 12                 | "Sin came into the world through one man." (ESV)
v.9              | Micah       | 3       | 11                 | "Its priests teach for a price, its prophets practice divination for money." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "After two days... the third day" — Possible meanings: (1) Messianic: points to Christ's resurrection. (2) National: Israel's restoration after a period of suffering. (3) Both: prophetic layering. The NT treats "third day" as resurrection language (1 Corinthians 15:4 says "according to the scriptures" — possibly referencing this verse).
v.4              | KJV         | "goodness" (KJV) / "love" (ESV) — Hebrew "hesed" = covenant loyalty, steadfast love, loving-kindness. The same word God uses of His OWN love. The point: THEIR hesed evaporates; God's hesed endures forever.
v.5              | KJV         | "hewn them by the prophets" — Hebrew "chatsab" = to cut, quarry stone. God's word through prophets cuts like a chisel. "Slain by the words of my mouth" — God's word is a weapon (cf. Hebrews 4:12).
v.6              | KJV         | "mercy" (KJV) / "steadfast love" (ESV) — Hebrew "hesed." "Knowledge of God" — Hebrew "da'at Elohim" = experiential, relational knowing, not merely intellectual awareness.
v.7              | KJV         | "like men" (KJV) / "like Adam" (ESV) — Hebrew "ke'adam." Can mean "like Adam" (proper noun) or "like men" (humanity in general). Both translations are valid; ESV's choice emphasizes the parallel with the original fall.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Beautiful words without lasting change = morning dew (v.4). It's easy to
   feel moved in worship, to make promises in prayer, to have spiritual
   emotion that evaporates by Tuesday. God is not impressed by your feelings
   — He measures your FAITHFULNESS over time. Does your devotion last past
   the altar call?

2. God wants mercy MORE than sacrifice (v.6). You can have perfect church
   attendance, generous giving, daily Bible reading — and still miss what
   God actually wants: compassion for people, covenant loyalty, and intimate
   knowledge of HIM. Don't substitute religious performance for relational
   reality.

3. "The knowledge of God" is the goal (v.6). Not knowledge ABOUT God —
   knowledge OF God. Personal, experiential, intimate. All your Bible study,
   all your theological accuracy — is it producing genuine KNOWING of the
   living Person of God? Or are you an expert on a stranger?

4. "Like Adam they transgressed" (v.7). Every sin repeats Eden: given
   blessing, given boundaries, choosing rebellion. Recognize the pattern in
   your own life. Where has God given you good things, set loving
   boundaries, and you've crossed them anyway? The cure is the same as
   Eden's: confession and return.

5. "What shall I do with you?" (v.4). This is not a God of rage — this is a
   God of anguish. Your inconsistency GRIEVES God. He is not looking for a
   reason to punish; He is searching for substance in your devotion. Give
   Him something lasting.

7.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 7
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Israel is compared to a heated oven of unchecked passions and a half-baked cake — they mix with foreign nations, seek help from Egypt and Assyria instead of God, yet refuse to return to Him.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Half-Baked Cake and the
Silly Dove
Key Themes: Internal corruption, political instability, alliances
with pagan nations, self-deception and unawareness of decay, God's
frustrated love

Historical Context: Chapter 7 continues the indictment of Israel
with two vivid metaphors: a cake not turned (burned on one side,
raw on the other) and a silly dove (flitting between Egypt and
Assyria). The chapter describes the internal political chaos of
Israel's final decades — kings assassinated, princes drunk,
conspiracies burning "like an oven." Between 746-732 BC, four
of Israel's last six kings were murdered by their successors. Yet
through all this chaos, "none among them calleth unto me" (v.7).
The deepest tragedy: God WANTED to heal them (v.1), trained and
strengthened them (v.15), yet they devised evil against Him.

Structure:
- God's Frustrated Healing: Iniquity Exposed (vv.1-2)
- The Oven: Political Conspiracy and Regicide (vv.3-7)
- The Half-Baked Cake: Unaware Decay (vv.8-10)
- The Silly Dove: Flitting Between Empires (vv.11-12)
- God's Lament: I Would Redeem, But They Rebel (vv.13-16)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | 2 Kings     | 15      | 10-25              | The series of assassinations and regicides in Israel's final years
v.8              | Revelation  | 3       | 15-16              | "Because you are lukewarm, and neither hot nor cold, I will spit you out of my mouth." (ESV)
v.8              | James       | 1       | 8                  | "He is a double-minded man, unstable in all his ways." (ESV)
v.9              | Judges      | 16      | 20                 | Samson: "He did not know that the LORD had left him." (ESV) Same unawareness
v.11             | Isaiah      | 30      | 1-7                | Woe to those who go to Egypt for help
v.11             | Isaiah      | 31      | 1                  | "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help and rely on horses." (ESV)
v.13             | Deuteronomy | 7       | 8                  | "The LORD...redeemed you out of the house of slavery." (ESV) The original redemption they forgot
v.14             | Joel        | 2       | 13                 | "Rend your hearts and not your garments." (ESV) God wants internal, not external, grief
v.16             | Psalm       | 78      | 57                 | "They turned away and acted treacherously like their fathers; they twisted like a deceitful bow." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | KJV         | "oven" — Hebrew "tannur" = a cylindrical clay oven. The repeated oven imagery (vv.4, 6, 7) pictures conspirators whose passions smolder overnight and blaze in the morning. The baker (perhaps the ringleader) lets the heat build.
v.5              | KJV         | "bottles of wine" (KJV) / "heat of wine" (ESV) — Hebrew "chamat yayin" = the fever/inflammation of wine. The princes get the king drunk on coronation day — vulnerability for assassination.
v.8              | KJV         | "cake not turned" — An unturned flatbread on hot stones: charred black on the bottom, raw dough on top. Completely unusable. Ephraim is half-cooked religion: outwardly burned by the world, inwardly uncooked by God's truth.
v.11             | KJV         | "silly" — Hebrew "potah" = naive, gullible, easily deceived. "Without heart" = without sense/discernment. The dove is proverbially foolish — flying into traps.
v.16             | KJV         | "deceitful bow" (KJV) / "treacherous bow" (ESV) — Hebrew "qeshet remiyyah" = a bow that misfires, aims at one target and hits another. Israel aims at God but hits everything else. Unreliable, untrustworthy.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "He knoweth it not" (v.9). This is the scariest phrase in the chapter —
   applied TWICE. You can be losing spiritual strength, aging in your faith,
   being consumed by the world — and NOT KNOW IT. Ask God to show you your
   gray hairs. Ask trusted people to tell you what you cannot see.
   Self-awareness requires external input.

2. Are you a "cake not turned"? (v.8). Half-committed. Burned on one side by
   worldly exposure, raw on the other from neglecting God's refining. Not
   fully useful to God or satisfying to the world. The solution: let God
   TURN you. Submit every side of your life to His heat.

3. Crying on your bed is not the same as crying to God (v.14). Emotional
   pain, sleepless nights, anxiety — these are NOT prayer unless directed TO
   God FROM the heart. Self-pity is not repentance. Redirect your distress
   GOD-ward.

4. God trains you, strengthens you — then watches to see what you do with it
   (v.15). Every talent, resource, opportunity, strength — it came from God.
   Are you using God-given capacities to serve God or to serve yourself? To
   bless others or to rebel?

5. "I would heal... I would redeem" (vv.1, 13). God's desire is ALWAYS
   toward healing and redemption. The obstacle is never His willingness. It
   is always our resistance. Stop blocking what God wants to give you.

8.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 8
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Israel has sown the wind and will reap the whirlwind — they have broken God's covenant, set up unauthorized kings and idols, and will be swallowed up among the nations.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — Sowing the Wind, Reaping the
Whirlwind
Key Themes: Covenant violation, self-made religion, sowing and
reaping, forgotten Maker, useless among the nations, the folly
of idolatry

Historical Context: Chapter 8 sounds the trumpet alarm — judgment
is swooping like a vulture. Israel claims to know God ("My God,
we know thee!" v.2) but has violated every aspect of the covenant:
making kings without God's direction, creating idols from God's
gold, building altars that multiply sin rather than atone for it.
The famous proverb of v.7 ("sow the wind, reap the whirlwind")
captures the chapter's message: actions have consequences far
exceeding their initial scope. Israel has "forgotten his Maker"
(v.14) — the ultimate cause of every other failure.

Structure:
- The Trumpet Alarm: Judgment Approaches (vv.1-3)
- Self-Made Kings and Self-Made Gods (vv.4-6)
- Sowing the Wind, Reaping the Whirlwind (vv.7-8)
- A Wild Donkey: Hiring Lovers (vv.9-10)
- Multiplied Altars, Multiplied Sin (vv.11-13)
- Israel Has Forgotten His Maker (v.14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Deuteronomy | 28      | 49                 | "The LORD will bring a nation against you from far away...like an eagle swooping down." (ESV)
v.4              | 1 Samuel    | 8       | 5-7                | Israel demanding a king without God's heart; God saying "they have rejected me."
v.5              | 1 Kings     | 12      | 28-30              | Jeroboam's golden calves at Bethel and Dan — the origin of Samaria's calf
v.7              | Galatians   | 6       | 7                  | "Whatever one sows, that will he also reap." (ESV)
v.7              | Job         | 4       | 8                  | "Those who plow iniquity and sow trouble reap the same." (ESV)
v.12             | Psalm       | 119     | 18                 | "Open my eyes, that I may behold wondrous things out of your law." (ESV) The correct response to God's written Word
v.13             | 1 Samuel    | 15      | 22                 | "To obey is better than sacrifice." (ESV)
v.14             | Deuteronomy | 32      | 18                 | "You were unmindful of the Rock that bore you, and you forgot the God who gave you birth." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "eagle" (KJV) / "vulture" (ESV) — Hebrew "nesher" can mean eagle or vulture. A vulture circling over prey is more fitting here — judgment circling, waiting to descend.
v.5              | KJV         | "Thy calf, O Samaria" — The golden calf set up by Jeroboam I at Bethel and Dan (1 Kings 12:28). Still being worshipped 200 years later. Sin's monuments outlive their makers.
v.7              | KJV         | "sown the wind" — Hebrew "ruach" (wind/spirit). "Whirlwind" — Hebrew "suphah" = storm, tempest. The harvest is exponentially larger than the planting. Wind becomes tornado.
v.9              | KJV         | "wild ass alone by himself" (KJV) / "wild donkey wandering alone" (ESV) — Hebrew "pere" = wild donkey. Israel has gone feral — untamed, alone, unteachable. "Hired lovers" = paying tribute to Assyria for protection.
v.12             | KJV         | "great things of my law" — Some translate "ten thousand things" (multiplied commandments). Even if God wrote infinitely more instruction, Israel would still treat it as foreign. The problem is not insufficient revelation but hardened hearts.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Saying "I know God" while living against Him is self-deception (v.2).
   Words mean nothing without matching conduct. Do your ACTIONS verify your
   profession? "Not every one that saith unto me, Lord, Lord" (Matthew
   7:21).

2. "Sow the wind, reap the whirlwind" (v.7). Every compromise multiplies.
   That "small" sin, that "minor" shortcut, that "little" disobedience — it
   will grow exponentially. You never harvest less than you plant. You
   always harvest MORE. Stop sowing breezes of compromise before the storm
   arrives.

3. Do you treat God's Word as "a strange thing"? (v.12). When Scripture
   seems irrelevant, foreign, outdated, or impractical — that is the symptom
   of a heart drifting from its Maker. The Word feels "strange" to those who
   have made themselves strangers to God.

4. "Israel has forgotten his Maker" (v.14). Not rejected — FORGOT. The slow
   drift is more dangerous than the dramatic rebellion. Busy building your
   career, your house, your security — and quietly forgetting the One who
   made you. Busyness is the most effective form of forgetfulness.

5. A "useless vessel" (v.8) — once chosen, now purposeless. A vessel that
   doesn't serve its maker has no function. If you are not fulfilling God's
   purpose, you are not just idle — you are lost among the nations. Purpose
   comes from the Potter, not from the pot.

9.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 9
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God announces the end of Israel's celebrations — exile is coming as punishment for their deep corruption, and the prophet himself is mocked as a fool by the people he warns.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Days of Punishment Have Come
Key Themes: False joy in harvest festivals, exile from the LORD's
land, barrenness as judgment, God's departure, wanderers among
nations, past love turned to present grief

Historical Context: Chapter 9 may have been delivered at a harvest
festival where Israel was celebrating like the pagan nations. Hosea
interrupts their joy: "Rejoice not!" (v.1). Their agricultural
prosperity (threshing floors, wine vats) was credited to Baal
worship; God will remove it. The chapter announces exile — they
will leave "the LORD's land" (v.3), eat unclean food in Assyria,
and be unable to worship properly. God recalls when He first found
Israel — "like grapes in the wilderness" (v.10) — precious and
delightful. But at Baal-peor they corrupted themselves. Now the
consequences: barrenness, bereavement, wandering among nations.

Structure:
- No Joy for Israel: The Harvest Will Fail (vv.1-6)
- The Days of Punishment Are Here (vv.7-9)
- God's Memory: Once Beloved, Now Corrupt (v.10)
- Ephraim's Glory Flies Away: Barrenness (vv.11-14)
- Driven Out of God's House (vv.15-17)

v.7              | Luke        | 19      | 44                 | "You did not know the time of your visitation." (ESV) — Jesus uses identical language of Jerusalem
v.9              | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Judges 19-20 — "The days of Gibeah" — the rape and murder of the Levite's concubine; Israel's lowest point before the monarchy.
v.10             | Numbers     | 25      | 1-5                | Baal-peor: Israel's sexual idolatry with Moabite women; 24,000 died
v.10             | Psalm       | 106     | 28                 | "They attached themselves to the Baal of Peor and ate sacrifices offered to the dead." (ESV)
v.12             | 1 Samuel    | 4       | 21                 | "Ichabod...The glory has departed from Israel." (ESV) Same theme: God's departure
v.17             | Deuteronomy | 28      | 64-65              | "The LORD will scatter you among all peoples...and among these nations you shall find no respite." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "loved a reward upon every cornfloor" (KJV) / "loved a prostitute's wages on all threshing floors" (ESV) — Israel treated harvest bounty as payment for prostitution with Baal — "wages" for worshipping fertility gods at threshing floors (common sites for pagan rituals).
v.7              | KJV         | "the prophet is a fool, the spiritual man is mad" — Either: (1) Israel calls the TRUE prophet a fool (mocking Hosea), or (2) the FALSE prophets are revealed as fools. Both readings are valid; context suggests the first.
v.10             | KJV         | "firstripe in the fig tree" — Hebrew "bikkurah" = the very first fig of the season, considered a delicacy (Micah 7:1). God's delight in young Israel was like eagerly eating the first fruit. Now that delight has turned to grief.
v.10             | KJV         | "became detestable like the thing they loved" — Hebrew "shiqquts" = detestable thing/abomination. A profound principle: you become like what you worship (Psalm 115:8).
v.15             | KJV         | "Gilgal" — Once the place of Israel's circumcision and renewal (Joshua 5), now corrupted into a center of false worship. Sacred places can become sinful places.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. You become what you worship (v.10). "They became detestable like the
   thing they loved." Whatever you give your devotion to SHAPES you. Worship
   God, become godly. Worship money, become greedy. Worship pleasure, become
   enslaved. Guard what receives your love — it determines what you become.

2. "Woe to them when I depart" (v.12). God's PRESENCE is the only thing that
   makes life work. When He withdraws, everything collapses — not because of
   external attack, but internal emptiness. Protect His presence in your
   life above all else.

3. God remembers what you WERE (v.10). "I found Israel like grapes in the
   wilderness." God recalls your early love, your first devotion, the
   freshness of your faith. He grieves the contrast with what you've become.
   Return to the delight you once were.

4. The prophet as "fool" (v.7). When truth-tellers are mocked as crazy, that
   itself is a sign of judgment. A culture that calls prophets fools is a
   culture under divine sentence. Don't join the mockers.

5. "Wanderers among the nations" (v.17). To live without God is to live
   without HOME. You may have an address, but without God you are a wanderer
   — directionless, restless, displaced. Only IN God are you truly settled.

10.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 10
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Israel is a luxuriant vine that produced fruit for itself — their prosperity led to more altars to false gods, and now God calls them to break up fallow ground and sow righteousness before judgment falls.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — Sow Righteousness or Reap
the Whirlwind
Key Themes: Prosperity misused for idolatry, divided hearts,
fallow ground, sowing and reaping, trusting in military might,
the call to seek the LORD

Historical Context: Chapter 10 continues the agricultural imagery.
Israel is a "luxuriant vine" (ESV) / "empty vine" (KJV) — fruitful
materially but spiritually barren. The more prosperous they became,
the more altars they built to false gods. Their heart is "divided"
(v.2) — attempting to serve both God and Baal. The calf of Beth-
aven (Bethel) will be carried off to Assyria as tribute. In the
midst of judgment oracles, verse 12 shines as one of the most
powerful calls to repentance in all the prophets: "Break up your
fallow ground: for it is time to seek the LORD."

Structure:
- The Luxuriant Vine: Prosperity Breeds Idolatry (vv.1-2)
- Kingless and Helpless (vv.3-4)
- The Calf Departs: Idols Carried Away (vv.5-8)
- The Call: Sow Righteousness, Seek the LORD (vv.9-12)
- The Harvest of Wickedness (vv.13-15)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Isaiah      | 5       | 1-7                | The vineyard song: Israel as God's vine that produced wild grapes
v.2              | 1 Kings     | 18      | 21                 | Elijah: "How long will you go limping between two different opinions?" (ESV) The same divided heart
v.8              | Luke        | 23      | 30                 | Jesus quotes this verse on the way to the cross, applied to Jerusalem's coming judgment
v.8              | Revelation  | 6       | 16                 | "Calling to the mountains and rocks, 'Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who is seated on the throne.'" (ESV)
v.12             | Jeremiah    | 4       | 3                  | "Break up your fallow ground, and sow not among thorns." (ESV) Same agricultural metaphor for repentance
v.12             | 2 Chronicles | 7       | 14                 | "If my people...humble themselves, and pray and seek my face...then I will hear from heaven." (ESV)
v.13             | Galatians   | 6       | 7-8                | "Whatever one sows, that will he also reap." (ESV)
v.13             | Proverbs    | 14      | 12                 | "There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way to death." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "empty vine" (KJV) vs. "luxuriant vine" (ESV) — Hebrew "baqaq" is debated: can mean "emptying" (pouring out fruit for itself) or "luxuriant/spreading." The ESV emphasizes Israel's fruitfulness that is self-directed; KJV emphasizes the spiritual emptiness despite outward production.
v.5              | KJV         | "Beth-aven" = "house of wickedness/vanity" — Hosea's sarcastic name for Bethel ("house of God"), which housed Jeroboam's golden calf.
v.8              | KJV         | "Aven" = shortened form of Beth-aven. "High places" = Hebrew "bamot" = hilltop shrines for pagan worship.
v.11             | KJV         | "heifer that is taught" (KJV) / "trained calf" (ESV) — Ephraim was once a domesticated, useful animal that loved to thresh (easy work: eating while treading grain). Now God will put her to harder labor — plowing and harrowing.
v.12             | KJV         | "fallow ground" — Hebrew "nir" = land left unplowed, hardened by neglect. Requires breaking (deep plowing) before it can receive seed. A metaphor for hearts hardened by spiritual neglect.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Prosperity can be your greatest spiritual danger (v.1). The MORE Israel
   had, the MORE they sinned. Success without God produces idolatry, not
   gratitude. Check: as your life improves, is your worship increasing or
   decreasing?

2. A divided heart is a false heart (v.2). You cannot maintain loyalty to
   God and to the world simultaneously. The attempt itself is faithlessness.
   Choose. "Purify your hearts, ye double minded" (James 4:8).

3. "Break up your fallow ground" (v.12). Hard-heartedness develops through
   neglect — seasons where you don't pray, don't read, don't respond to
   conviction. The ground of your heart becomes hard and unresponsive. BREAK
   IT UP. Repentance is the plow. Confession is the blade. Let God's Word
   penetrate again.

4. "It is TIME to seek the LORD" (v.12). Urgency. Not tomorrow. Not when
   circumstances improve. Not when you feel like it. NOW. The time to seek
   God is always NOW. Every delay makes the ground harder.

5. You always eat what you plant (v.13). Plowed wickedness? You'll eat lies.
   Sowed righteousness? You'll reap steadfast love. Life is a farm. What are
   you planting TODAY?

11.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 11
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God recalls His tender love for Israel as a child — teaching them to walk, healing them, drawing them with cords of kindness — yet they turned away, and now His compassion wrestles with His justice.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Father's Love for His
Rebellious Child
Key Themes: God as Father, the pain of unrequited parental love,
divine compassion overcoming divine wrath, "I am God and not man,"
the call from Egypt, future restoration

Historical Context: Chapter 11 is often considered the emotional
CLIMAX of Hosea — shifting from husband/wife imagery to FATHER/
CHILD. God recalls teaching infant Israel to walk, holding him
by the arms, feeding him, leading with "cords of kindness." Yet
the more God called, the more Israel ran away. This leads to
God's most anguished self-conflict: "How can I give you up?"
(v.8). The answer reveals God's very nature: "I am God, and not
man" — His compassion is DIVINE, not human. A human father might
give up; God cannot. This chapter quotes the Exodus ("out of Egypt
I called my son") — applied by Matthew to Jesus (Matthew 2:15).

Structure:
- God's Fatherly Love Recalled (vv.1-4)
- The Consequence: Exile Under Assyria (vv.5-7)
- God's Heart Torn: "How Can I Give You Up?" (vv.8-9)
- Future Restoration: The Lion Roars (vv.10-11)
- Ephraim's Current State: Lies (v.12)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Matthew     | 2       | 15                 | "Out of Egypt I called my son" (ESV) — applied to Jesus' return from Egypt as an infant
v.1              | Exodus      | 4       | 22                 | "Israel is my firstborn son." (ESV)
v.3              | Deuteronomy | 1       | 31                 | "The LORD your God carried you, as a man carries his son." (ESV)
v.4              | John        | 6       | 44                 | "No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him." (ESV) God DRAWS — same concept
v.4              | Jeremiah    | 31      | 3                  | "I have loved you with an everlasting love; therefore I have continued my faithfulness to you." (ESV)
v.8              | Genesis     | 19      | 24-25              | Admah and Zeboiim destroyed with Sodom and Gomorrah (Deuteronomy 29:23)
v.8              | Isaiah      | 63      | 15                 | "Where are your zeal and your might? The stirring of your inner parts and your compassion?" (ESV)
v.9              | Numbers     | 23      | 19                 | "God is not man, that he should lie." (ESV) His constancy exceeds human capacity
v.9              | Malachi     | 3       | 6                  | "For I the LORD do not change; therefore you, O children of Jacob, are not consumed." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "called my son out of Egypt" — Historically: the Exodus. Typologically: Christ's return from Egypt. Matthew sees Jesus RECAPITULATING Israel's story — living out what Israel was meant to be.
v.4              | KJV         | "cords of a man" (KJV) / "cords of kindness" (ESV) — Hebrew "chablei adam." "Adam" = man/humanity. God draws with HUMAN cords — gentle, relational, personal — not with divine force. He condescends to lead at our level.
v.8              | KJV         | "Admah... Zeboiim" — Cities on the plain of Sodom destroyed by God (Deuteronomy 29:23). God is asking: "Can I do THAT to you?" And the answer from His own heart is: NO.
v.8              | KJV         | "mine heart is turned" (KJV) / "my heart recoils" (ESV) — Hebrew "nehpak" = overturned, reversed. God's heart literally TURNS OVER within Him. Anthropomorphic language expressing divine emotion that transcends our categories.
v.9              | KJV         | "I am God, and not man" — Hebrew "El anochi v'lo ish." The theological foundation of divine faithfulness: God's nature is fundamentally different from ours. Human love fails; divine love endures.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God taught you to walk (v.3). Every spiritual milestone — your first
   prayer, your first act of faith, your first step of obedience — God was
   there, holding your arms, teaching you. And you probably "knew not." Look
   back and recognize His hand.

2. "Cords of kindness, bands of love" (v.4). God does not coerce. He draws.
   He woos. He leads with kindness. If your picture of God is a tyrant with
   chains, you have the wrong picture. He STOOPS to feed you. He eases your
   yoke.

3. "How can I give you up?" (v.8). This is a QUESTION, not a statement. God
   — the Almighty — expressing INNER CONFLICT over you. Your rebellion
   causes PAIN in the heart of God. Not cold judgment, but anguished love.
   Let that break you where theological arguments cannot.

4. "I am God, and not man" (v.9). Human love has limits. You might give up
   on someone who repeatedly betrays you. God CANNOT. His very nature
   prevents final abandonment of His people. Rest in this: the love holding
   you is not human love (which fails). It is divine love (which does not).

5. "They knew not that I healed them" (v.3). How many times has God healed
   you — physically, emotionally, relationally — and you attributed it to
   medicine, time, or luck? Ask God to open your eyes to His unrecognized
   kindnesses.

12.notes:
Hosea - Chapter 12
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God contrasts Israel's current deceitfulness with their ancestor Jacob, who wrestled with God and prevailed — calling them to return to God, practice loyalty and justice, and wait continually for Him.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Hosea son of Beeri. Prophesied to the northern kingdom
(Israel) approximately 755-715 BC. Contemporary with Isaiah and
Micah. Key theme: God's unfailing love for unfaithful Israel,
illustrated through Hosea's marriage to Gomer (a prostitute).
Despite Israel's spiritual adultery, God says: "I will heal their
backsliding, I will love them freely" (14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — Jacob's Example: Wrestling with
God vs. Deceiving Men
Key Themes: Feeding on wind, the Jacob story as model and warning,
false balances and self-deception, the ministry of prophets, the
call to return

Historical Context: Chapter 12 reaches back to the patriarch Jacob
as both WARNING and MODEL. Jacob the deceiver (heel-grabber) became
Israel the God-wrestler. God challenges Ephraim to follow Jacob's
BETTER example: weeping, seeking God's favor, encountering Him at
Bethel. Instead, Ephraim has become a dishonest merchant with
false balances, self-justified in wealth, feeding on the "wind"
of useless foreign alliances. The chapter highlights God's
consistent method: "by a prophet" He brought them out and "by a
prophet" He guards them still.

Structure:
- Ephraim Feeds on Wind: Useless Alliances (v.1)
- God's Case Against Jacob/Judah (vv.2-6)
- The Dishonest Merchant: Self-Deceived (vv.7-8)
- God's Consistency: Prophets and Visions (vv.9-11)
- The Jacob Parallel: Served for a Wife (vv.12-13)
- Ephraim's Bitter Provocation (v.14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3              | Genesis     | 25      | 26                 | "His hand held Esau's heel, so his name was called Jacob." (ESV)
v.4              | Genesis     | 32      | 24-30              | Jacob wrestles with God at Peniel and is renamed Israel
v.6              | Micah       | 6       | 8                  | "Do justice, love kindness, and walk humbly with your God." (ESV) — the same three requirements
v.7              | Amos        | 8       | 5                  | "Making the ephah small and the shekel great and dealing deceitfully with false balances." (ESV)
v.8              | Revelation  | 3       | 17                 | "You say, I am rich, I have prospered, and I need nothing, not realizing that you are wretched, pitiable, poor." (ESV)
v.10             | Hebrews     | 1       | 1                  | "Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets." (ESV) Same principle
v.13             | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Exodus 12-14 — "By a prophet" (Moses) God brought Israel out of Egypt.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "east wind" — Hebrew "qadim" = the sirocco, a devastating hot desert wind from the east that withers vegetation. A metaphor for Assyria (which approaches from the east).
v.4              | KJV         | "he wept, and made supplication" — Genesis 32 doesn't explicitly mention weeping, but Hosea adds this detail (perhaps by divine revelation or oral tradition). Jacob's wrestling was not defiant — it was desperate, tear-filled clinging to God.
v.7              | KJV         | "merchant" — Hebrew "kena'an" = literally "Canaan" or "Canaanite." The Canaanites were known as merchants/traders. Hosea is calling Ephraim a "Canaanite" — they have become indistinguishable from the pagans they were meant to displace.
v.9              | KJV         | "tabernacles/tents" — Reference to the Feast of Tabernacles (Booths). God will return them to tents — either as judgment (exile/wandering) or as renewed dependence (remembering wilderness trust).

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Feeding on wind" (v.1). What are you pursuing that gives no nourishment?
   Relationships, ambitions, habits that consume your time and energy but
   leave you empty? The east wind destroys; it doesn't sustain. Evaluate
   what you're chasing.

2. Jacob is both warning and example (vv.3-4). He schemed AND he wrestled
   with God. The question is: which Jacob are you? The deceiver with false
   balances, or the wrestler who weeps and won't let go until blessed? Be
   the second Jacob.

3. Wealth does not equal innocence (v.8). "I'm rich, therefore I'm
   righteous" is the oldest self-deception. Financial success says nothing
   about your spiritual condition. Some of the most prosperous people are
   the most spiritually bankrupt.

4. "Wait on thy God continually" (v.6). Patience is a command, not a
   suggestion. In a culture of instant gratification, God says: WAIT.
   Continually. Not just when it's convenient. The waiting itself is
   obedience.

5. God speaks in many ways (v.10). Prophets, visions, parables. If you claim
   God is silent, have you checked all His channels? Scripture, counsel,
   circumstances, conscience — He multiplies communication. The issue is not
   His silence but our deafness.
